required.title: [Read Aloud] The Story of the World, Volume 4: The Modern Age
required.author: Susan Wise Bauer

required.title: [For Mentor] A good world atlas or globe
[For Mentor] The Cold War: A New History
required.author: John Lewis Gaddis

suggested.title: [Read Aloud / Look Together] The Wall: Growing Up Behind the Iron Curtain
suggested.author: Peter Sis

suggested.title: [Look Together] Photographs and short videos of the Berlin Wall falling (November 9, 1989)
[For Mentor] News articles discussed at an appropriate level

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Postwar: A History of Europe Since 1945
supplementary.author: Tony Judt

competence_markers.description: The student can narrate the Cold War in basic terms — two sides, what they believed, how it ended
The student can explain the significance of the Berlin Wall and describe what happened when it fell
The student can describe 9/11 as a historical event and explain why it changed the world
The student understands that the internet changed how people communicate and that this created both benefits and dangers
The student is aware that China has become a major world power and can describe at least one way it differs from their own country
The student can identify at least one ongoing problem or injustice in the contemporary world and explain why it matters
The student demonstrates a basic understanding that the present is shaped by the past and that they are living inside history
The student engages with narration prompts with detail and personal interpretation, not rote recitation

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of the Cold War.
narration_prompts.detail: What were the two sides? What did they believe? How did they compete? How did it end?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the Berlin Wall.
narration_prompts.detail: Why was it built? What was it like to live with it? What happened when it fell? Why did people celebrate?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me what happened on September 11, 2001.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? What changed afterward? Why does it still matter?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about how the internet changed the world.
narration_prompts.detail: What was life like before it? What did it make possible? What problems did it create?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a problem in the world today that has not been solved.
narration_prompts.detail: Who is affected? Why hasn't it been fixed? What would need to happen?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why are people in many countries angry at their governments right now?
narration_prompts.detail: What has happened to make them feel this way? Do you think their anger makes sense?
What happened under Mao? What changed after him? What is China like today? How is it different from the country you live in?

cross_connections.description: The geographic literacy begun in 1.2.1 supports every topic in this packet — the student who knows where Russia, China, the Middle East, and Europe are can anchor contemporary events on a map.
The Cold War is a competition between two systems of government. The populism section connects directly to the question of what happens when governments fail the people they serve.
The internet and social media sections connect directly to the emerging skills of recognizing when someone is trying to manipulate you.
The digital revolution section connects to the practical skills of using technology wisely being developed in Domain 4.
Understanding 9/11 requires the beginning of understanding that people have very different beliefs about the world and act on them.
The patterns visible in this packet — institutional failure, populism, great-power competition — are the contemporary versions of patterns the student has seen across all of human history.

content.why_this_matters:
Everything you have learned so far in history — the first civilizations, the classical empires, the medieval world, the revolutions, the world wars — has led to the world you are living in right now. This packet is about the present. It is about the world outside your window.

The world you were born into was shaped by things that happened in your parents' and grandparents' lifetimes. Two powerful countries — the United States and the Soviet Union — spent decades competing over how the world should work. When that competition ended, the world changed dramatically. New technologies were invented that changed how people talk to each other, how they learn, how they buy things, and how they understand what is true. Wars were fought in new places for new reasons. Old problems that people thought were solved came back. New problems that nobody expected appeared.

You need to understand this world — not just live in it, but understand it. A person who does not understand why things are the way they are will believe whatever they are told about it. A person who does understand can think for themselves.

This packet will not tell you what to think about the world. It will tell you what happened, as honestly as possible, so you can begin forming your own understanding.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Understanding of the Cold War** — what the two superpowers believed, how they competed, and what the world looked like while they did
2. **Awareness of the contemporary world** — the student knows that countries have different governments, different histories, and different problems
3. **The internet as a civilizational shift** — understanding that a technology changed how all humans communicate and think
4. **Honest awareness of ongoing conflict and injustice** — the world still has wars, still has people who are treated unfairly, still has problems that have not been solved
5. **The idea that the present is also history** — the things happening now will be studied by future students just as we study Rome and Egypt
6. **Resistance to simple narratives** — the world is complicated, and anyone who tells you it is simple is leaving something out
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Two Superpowers — The Cold War

After World War II ended in 1945, the world did not become peaceful. It split into two sides.

On one side was the United States and its allies — countries that believed in democracy, private property, and individual freedom. These countries were not perfect. They had serious problems of their own. But they believed, generally, that people should be free to own things, start businesses, vote for their leaders, and speak their minds.

On the other side was the Soviet Union and its allies — countries run by communist governments. The communist idea, as you learned in 1.1.5, was that private property caused inequality, and that the government should own everything and distribute it fairly. In practice, this meant the government controlled almost all aspects of life. People could not freely leave. They could not freely speak. They could not freely choose their work or their leaders.

This competition was called the Cold War — "cold" because the two superpowers never directly fought each other in a full war. They were too afraid to, because both sides had nuclear weapons — bombs so powerful that a war between them could destroy the world. Instead, they competed in other ways: building bigger armies, racing to space, supporting opposite sides in smaller wars around the world, and trying to convince other countries to join their side.

The Cold War lasted from roughly 1947 to 1991. For over forty years, every person on Earth lived under the shadow of a possible nuclear war. This is hard to imagine now. But your grandparents grew up practicing hiding under their desks at school in case a nuclear bomb fell. That was the world.

### Part 2: The World the Cold War Shaped

The Cold War shaped the entire world — not just the United States and the Soviet Union.

**Europe was divided.** A wall was built through the middle of the city of Berlin, Germany, in 1961. On one side: freedom, prosperity, color. On the other side: government control, poverty, gray concrete. Families were separated. People who tried to cross the wall were shot. The Berlin Wall became the most powerful symbol of the Cold War — a wall that literally divided a city, a country, and a continent.

**Korea was divided.** After World War II, Korea was split into North Korea (communist, backed by the Soviet Union and China) and South Korea (democratic, backed by the United States). They fought a terrible war from 1950 to 1953. The border between them — the DMZ — still exists today. South Korea became one of the most prosperous countries on Earth. North Korea became one of the poorest, most isolated, and most oppressive.

**Countries around the world were pressured to choose a side.** In Africa, Asia, and Latin America, newly independent countries were courted, pressured, bribed, and sometimes invaded by both superpowers. Many of the wars and political problems in these regions today trace back to Cold War interference.

**The space race happened.** The Soviet Union launched the first satellite (Sputnik, 1957) and sent the first human into space (Yuri Gagarin, 1961). The United States responded by landing humans on the moon (Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin, 1969). The space race was competition, but it was also one of humanity's greatest achievements — we left our own planet for the first time.

### Part 3: Decolonization — Countries Becoming Free

While the Cold War was happening, something else enormous was happening around the world: dozens of countries were gaining their independence from European empires.

For centuries, European countries — Britain, France, Spain, Portugal, the Netherlands, Belgium — had controlled vast territories in Africa, Asia, and the Americas. They had taken resources, drawn borders, and ruled over millions of people who had no say in their own governance.

After World War II, this system began to collapse. One by one, countries demanded and gained their freedom. India gained independence from Britain in 1947. Ghana became the first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence in 1957. By the 1960s, dozens of new nations had appeared on the map.

This was a great and necessary thing — people governing themselves. But it was not simple. The borders the Europeans had drawn often made no sense — they cut across ethnic groups and lumped together peoples who had no shared history. The newly independent countries often had very little infrastructure, few trained administrators, and powerful neighbors or former colonial powers still trying to control them. Many struggled. Some are still struggling today.

Decolonization is not something that happened long ago. Its effects are visible everywhere in the world right now.

### Part 4: The Wall Falls — The End of the Cold War

On November 9, 1989, something happened that almost nobody predicted: the Berlin Wall fell.

The Soviet Union had been weakening for years. Its economy could not keep up with the West. Its people were tired of shortages, restrictions, and lies. A Soviet leader named Mikhail Gorbachev tried to reform the system — to make it more open and more honest. But once people were allowed to speak, they spoke. And what they said was: we want to be free.

Country after country in Eastern Europe broke away from Soviet control. Poland, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, Romania — people took to the streets, and the old communist governments fell. In Berlin, thousands of people climbed the wall, stood on top of it, and took sledgehammers to it. Families who had been separated for decades embraced in the rubble.

In 1991, the Soviet Union itself dissolved. The Cold War was over. The United States was the only superpower left.

This was a moment of extraordinary hope. Many people believed that democracy and freedom had won permanently — that the future would be peaceful, prosperous, and free. They were partly right and partly wrong.

### Part 5: September 11, 2001

On the morning of September 11, 2001, nineteen men hijacked four airplanes in the United States. They flew two of them into the World Trade Center towers in New York City. They flew one into the Pentagon in Washington, D.C. The fourth crashed in a field in Pennsylvania after passengers fought back.

Nearly 3,000 people died that morning.

The men who did this were members of a group called al-Qaeda, led by a man named Osama bin Laden. They believed — wrongly and terribly — that murdering innocent people was justified by their interpretation of Islam. Most Muslims around the world were horrified by what they did. Islam, like every major religion, has hundreds of millions of peaceful followers. But the attackers claimed to act in its name.

9/11 changed the world. The United States went to war in Afghanistan (where al-Qaeda was based) and then in Iraq. These wars lasted for twenty years. Hundreds of thousands of people died — most of them not Americans. Airport security changed. Government surveillance expanded. Fear of terrorism reshaped laws, politics, and daily life in ways that are still felt today.

What happened on 9/11 is important not just because of the attacks themselves, but because of everything that followed. The decisions made in fear and anger after that day shaped the world you were born into.

### Part 6: The Rise of China

While the United States was fighting wars in the Middle East, something else was happening on the other side of the world: China was becoming one of the most powerful countries on Earth.

China had been a communist country since 1949, when Mao Zedong's revolution succeeded. Under Mao, China suffered terribly — the Great Leap Forward (1958–1962) caused a famine that killed an estimated 45–55 million people, one of the worst disasters in all of human history. But after Mao died in 1976, new leaders began to change the economic system. They kept the communist political system — one party, no elections, no free speech — but they allowed people to start businesses, trade with other countries, and make money.

The result was astonishing. In a single generation, hundreds of millions of Chinese people were lifted out of poverty. China became the world's factory — making the clothes, electronics, toys, and tools that people around the world buy every day. China built cities, highways, high-speed trains, and skyscrapers at a speed and scale the world had never seen.

But China remained an authoritarian country. The government controls what people can say, what they can read online, and what they can protest. In 1989 — the same year the Berlin Wall fell — Chinese students protesting for democracy in Tiananmen Square were crushed by the military. The government still does not allow this to be discussed in China.

China's rise is one of the most important facts about the world you live in. It means the United States is no longer the only great power. The world has returned to competition between powerful countries with very different ideas about how people should live.

### Part 7: The European Union — An Experiment in Unity

After the devastation of two world wars, European countries tried something remarkable: they began joining together.

It started small — France and Germany agreeing to share control of coal and steel production so they could never again use those resources to build armies against each other. Over decades, more countries joined. The European Union eventually included 27 countries, with a shared currency (the euro), open borders, and common laws.

The idea was noble: if countries are bound together economically, they will not go to war with each other. And in many ways, it worked. Europe experienced its longest period of peace in recorded history.

But the EU also created problems. Decisions were increasingly made by officials in Brussels whom ordinary people had never elected and could not remove. Countries with very different economies — wealthy Germany and struggling Greece — shared a currency but not a budget. When problems arose, the system was slow to respond and often seemed to serve the interests of the largest, richest countries.

In 2016, the United Kingdom voted to leave the European Union — a decision called Brexit. It was a shock. It showed that many people felt the EU had gone too far, that they wanted their country to govern itself. Whether Brexit was wise or foolish is still being argued. What matters here is the pattern: people gave up self-governance for the promise of prosperity and peace, and some of them wanted it back.

### Part 8: The 2008 Financial Crisis

In 2008, the world's economy nearly collapsed.

Banks in the United States had been making risky loans — lending money to people who could not pay it back, then selling those bad loans to other banks as if they were safe investments. When the truth came out, banks failed. Stock markets crashed. Millions of people lost their homes, their jobs, and their savings.

Governments rescued the banks — spending trillions of dollars of taxpayers' money to keep the financial system from collapsing entirely. The people who ran the banks, who had caused the crisis through greed and recklessness, were mostly not punished. Many of them kept their fortunes. Ordinary people suffered the consequences.

This mattered enormously for trust. Many people concluded — not unreasonably — that the system was rigged: when ordinary people make mistakes, they suffer the consequences; when powerful institutions make mistakes, they get rescued. This anger never went away. It is still shaping politics today.

### Part 9: The Digital Revolution

The internet changed everything. This is not an exaggeration.

Before the internet, information was controlled by a small number of gatekeepers — newspapers, television networks, publishers, universities. If they did not tell you something, you probably would not learn it. If they told you something false, you had very little way to check.

The internet broke that system open. Suddenly, anyone could publish. Anyone could find information. Anyone could communicate with anyone else on the planet, instantly. This was liberating. It gave ordinary people access to knowledge that had been locked away in expensive libraries and exclusive institutions.

But the internet also created new problems. When anyone can publish, anyone includes liars, manipulators, and propagandists. When algorithms decide what you see based on what keeps you clicking, you end up seeing whatever makes you angry or afraid — because anger and fear keep people clicking. Social media was designed to capture your attention, not to make you informed or wise.

The result: people today have more information available to them than any humans in history, and many of them are more confused, more angry, and more divided than ever. The tool is extraordinary. Learning to use it well — and to resist its manipulations — is one of the most important skills you can develop.

### Part 10: COVID and the Trust Crisis

In late 2019, a new virus appeared in Wuhan, China. By early 2020, it had spread across the world. Governments shut down schools, businesses, and public life. People were told to stay home. Masks were required. Borders closed.

COVID-19 killed millions of people worldwide. But the pandemic also revealed something that may matter even more in the long run: many people did not trust their governments, their health officials, or their media to tell them the truth about what was happening.

Some of this distrust was irrational. But some of it was earned. Officials changed their guidance repeatedly. Some things people were told — about the origins of the virus, about whether masks worked, about the risks of vaccines — turned out to be incomplete, oversimplified, or wrong. People who asked questions were sometimes called dangerous. Debate was shut down. And when the official story changed, those same officials rarely acknowledged their earlier mistakes.

The lesson is not that all authorities lie. The lesson is that trust is earned through honesty, and institutions that demand trust while being dishonest will lose it — and deserve to.

### Part 11: The Return of Great-Power Competition

For about twenty years after the Soviet Union fell, the United States was the most powerful country in the world by a wide margin. Some people called this the "unipolar moment" — one pole of power instead of two.

That moment is over.

China has become a military and economic rival. Russia, under Vladimir Putin, invaded Ukraine in 2022 — the largest war in Europe since World War II. Other countries — India, Turkey, Saudi Arabia, Brazil — are asserting their own power and refusing to simply follow the United States.

The world is becoming multipolar again — multiple centers of power competing with each other. This is not necessarily bad, but it is dangerous. It means the world is less predictable. Alliances shift. Conflicts become possible in places that seemed stable. The rules that governed international behavior after World War II are being tested.

Understanding this is essential. You are growing up in a world where great powers are competing again, and the outcome is not decided.

### Part 12: Populism and Institutional Failure

In country after country in recent years, people have voted for leaders who promise to fight against the existing system — against the politicians, the experts, the media, and the institutions that have run things for decades.

This is called populism, and it appears on both the left and the right. Its core message is: the people in charge have failed you, and I will fight for you against them.

Sometimes populist leaders are genuine reformers who challenge real corruption. Sometimes they are demagogues who exploit anger without solving anything. Usually they are some mixture of both.

What matters is the underlying question: **Why are so many people in so many countries angry at their institutions?** The answer is not simple, but the pattern is real. In many countries, wages have stagnated while the cost of living has risen. The 2008 financial crisis showed that the system protects the powerful. The COVID response showed that experts can be wrong and defensive about it. Immigration has changed communities in ways that some people welcome and others resent. The internet has made it possible for people to see, for the first time, exactly how differently their leaders live from how they live.

Populism is a symptom. The disease is institutional failure — institutions that stopped serving the people they were supposed to serve. Understanding this is the beginning of understanding contemporary politics.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Read-Through [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Read the relevant chapters of *The Story of the World, Volume 4* aloud, supplemented by the core content sections above. After each reading, the student narrates back what they heard. Supplement with maps, photographs, and short video clips where appropriate — the visual record of the contemporary era is rich and accessible.

### Study 2: The Cold War Map Project [Early in the Packet]
On a world map, color or mark the countries that were allied with the United States (NATO) and the countries allied with the Soviet Union (Warsaw Pact). Mark the Berlin Wall. Mark Korea's division. Mark other Cold War conflicts the student has learned about. This map makes the Cold War concrete and geographic, not abstract.

### Study 3: The Wall Falls [Mid-Packet]
Watch footage of the Berlin Wall falling together. Look at photographs. Read accounts from people who were there. Discussion: What did the wall mean? Why were people so happy? What does it feel like to be free after not being free? What do you think happened the next day — was everything immediately better?

### Study 4: Understanding 9/11 [Mid-Packet]
Read *14 Cows for America* together. Discuss what happened on September 11th at an age-appropriate level. Key questions: What happened? Why did the attackers do it? Was their reason a good reason? What changed afterward? Why did a village in Kenya send cows to America — what does that tell you about how people respond to tragedy?

### Study 5: Your Technology [Mid-to-Late Packet]
A practical study: How does the student use technology? What do they use the internet for? What do they watch? How do they decide what is true and what is not? This is not a lecture — it is a conversation. The goal is to begin building awareness that technology is a tool, not an authority, and that what appears on a screen was put there by someone for a reason.

### Study 6: People Who Are Still Treated Unfairly [Late Packet]
A dedicated study of ongoing injustice: Roma communities in Europe still face discrimination in housing, education, and employment. Refugees from wars in Syria, Ukraine, and elsewhere live in camps. Indigenous peoples in many countries still fight for their rights. The student should understand that history is not something that only happened in the past — it is happening now, to real people.

### Study 7: Timeline Extension [End of Packet]
Add to the physical timeline begun in earlier packets: the Cold War, the Berlin Wall falling, 9/11, the rise of China, COVID, the invasion of Ukraine. The student sees that recent history is packed with events — and that they are living inside history right now.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Cold War Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can explain the basic structure — two superpowers, different beliefs, nuclear weapons preventing direct war, competition everywhere else. Can explain how it ended — the Soviet Union weakened and dissolved. Uses specific details: the Berlin Wall, the space race, the division of Korea.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot distinguish the Cold War from World War II. No sense of what the competition was about or how it played out.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the map project done? Geographic anchoring often makes the abstract concrete. Has the student seen the Berlin Wall footage? Visual evidence is powerful at this age.

### 2. Contemporary Awareness
**Developing well:** The student knows that the world has multiple powerful countries, ongoing conflicts, and unresolved problems. Can name at least a few: China's rise, Russia's invasion of Ukraine, the refugee crisis, Roma discrimination. Does not treat the present as a finished story.

**Developing slowly:** The student treats the modern world as background — something that just "is" without causes or dynamics. No curiosity about why things are the way they are.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you having conversations about current events as they arise? A student who never hears adults discuss the world honestly will not develop the habit of thinking about it.

### 3. Technology Awareness
**Developing well:** The student understands that the internet changed how people communicate and that this creates both opportunities and dangers. Can articulate at least one benefit (information access) and one risk (manipulation, misinformation). Beginning to think critically about their own technology use.

**Developing slowly:** The student treats the internet as a natural feature of the world, like air — no sense that it was invented, that it changed things, or that it can be used well or poorly.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you had the Study 5 conversation about the student's own technology use? This is one of the most practically important discussions in the packet.

### 4. Emotional Engagement Without Overwhelm
**Developing well:** The student can engage with difficult topics — 9/11, COVID, war — with appropriate seriousness but without being frightened or overwhelmed. Asks questions. Expresses their own thoughts and feelings. Understands that bad things happen in the world and that understanding them is better than ignoring them.

**Developing slowly:** The student is either unaffected (not engaging emotionally) or distressed (overwhelmed by the material). Either extreme suggests the calibration needs adjustment — lighter touch for the overwhelmed student, more concrete and personal framing for the disengaged one.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Editorializing Instead of Teaching
This packet's topics are politically charged. You have opinions about COVID policy, about the war in Ukraine, about populism, about the financial crisis. Those opinions are fine to have. But the student's first encounter with these topics should be with the facts and the honest complexity, not with your conclusions. Tell the story. Let the student react. If they ask what you think, share your view — but also share that other intelligent people disagree, and explain why.

The goal is not neutrality (which is impossible and dishonest) but honesty: here is what happened, here is why people disagree about it, and here is why understanding it matters more than picking a side at age seven.

### Shielding Instead of Calibrating
Some mentors will want to skip 9/11, or the 2008 crisis, or COVID — because these topics feel too heavy for young children. Do not skip them. Calibrate them. A four-year-old does not need to know the death toll of 9/11. But they can understand that something very bad happened, that it changed the world, and that the people who did it believed something that led them to hurt innocent people. The story of 14 Cows for America is the perfect entry point — it starts with human kindness, not with terror.

### Making the Present Feel Settled
The past feels settled because we know what happened next. The present does not have that luxury. Do not present any contemporary situation as resolved or inevitable. The rise of China is not a settled trajectory — it could change. The European Union could strengthen or weaken. The war in Ukraine could end in many ways. The student should understand that the present is genuinely open — that what happens next depends on what people decide and do.

### Ignoring Ongoing Injustice
It is easier to teach about injustice in the past than in the present. Past injustice is safely distant; present injustice implies a responsibility to do something. Study 6 — on Roma discrimination, refugees, and indigenous rights — should not be rushed or treated as an afterthought. The student needs to understand that the moral challenges of history are not over. Some of them are happening right now, to real people, and indifference to them is a choice.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: The Cold War
**Strong:** Describes both sides and what they believed. Includes specific details — nuclear weapons, the wall, the space race. Explains how it ended. May include personal reactions or questions.
**Weak:** "America and Russia didn't like each other." No structure, no mechanism, no resolution.

### Prompt 2: The Berlin Wall
**Strong:** Describes the wall — what it meant, what life was like on each side, why people celebrated when it fell. May connect to the idea of freedom.
**Weak:** "There was a wall and it came down." No sense of what the wall meant or why it mattered.

### Prompt 3: September 11
**Strong:** Describes what happened with appropriate seriousness. Explains that it changed the world — wars followed, security changed, fear increased. May express their own feelings about it.
**Weak:** "Bad guys crashed planes." No context, no consequences, no engagement.

### Prompt 4: The Internet
**Strong:** Describes both benefits (access to information, communication) and dangers (misinformation, manipulation, attention capture). May connect to their own experience with technology.
**Weak:** "The internet lets you look stuff up." No awareness of its significance as a civilizational change.

### Prompt 5: An Ongoing Problem
**Strong:** Identifies a specific problem — Roma discrimination, refugees, a current conflict — and explains who is affected and why it persists. Shows awareness that the world still has unsolved problems.
**Weak:** Cannot name any ongoing problem. Or names one but cannot explain anything about it.

### Prompt 6: Anger at Institutions
**Strong:** Can explain at least one reason people are angry — the financial crisis, COVID response failures, wages not keeping up — and express whether they think the anger makes sense. Shows the beginning of political awareness.
**Weak:** Has no framework for understanding why people might distrust institutions.

### Prompt 7: China
**Strong:** Knows about Mao's catastrophe. Knows about the economic transformation. Can articulate at least one difference between China's system and their own country's. Shows awareness that China is a major world power.
**Weak:** "China makes stuff." No historical depth, no political awareness.
